product: Cluely (AI Desktop Assistant for Video Conferencing Coaching)

system_description.overview: Cluely, Inc. operates an AI-powered desktop assistant for video conferencing coaching, targeting sales professionals. The platform integrates with Zoom, Microsoft Teams, Google Meet, WebEx, and GoToMeeting to provide real-time coaching and analysis during calls. The system uses AI (via Deepgram for speech-to-text) with a multi-provider architecture spanning Vercel, Cloudflare, AWS, and Neon.

products.Cluely AI Desktop Assistant: Desktop application providing real-time AI coaching during video conferencing calls; supports Zoom, Microsoft Teams, Google Meet, WebEx, GoToMeeting; sales performance and coaching focus

infrastructure.cloud_provider: Vercel (primary subservice organization - carve-out; Vercel has its own SOC 2 report). AWS also used (Kubernetes cluster for Deepgram AI engines - not named as subservice organization). Cloudflare used for edge computing/CDN/queuing. Neon used for database.
regions: Not specified by name; multi-availability zone deployment confirmed in Vercel
infrastructure.physical_presence: None - no physical offices; operates from working space managed by building management
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (disaster recovery via 'spinning up multiple servers across all availability zones in Vercel')
additional_cloud: AWS (Kubernetes cluster with Deepgram speech AI engines)
serverless: Cloudflare Workers (queue processing - queue 1 and queue 2 visible in network diagram)
infrastructure.cdn: Cloudflare (shown in network diagram as proxy/CDN layer between Vercel API platform and Neon database)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: Hosted in Vercel in a virtual private cloud (VPC) environment with segmented VPCs and access control lists (ACLs). Network segmentation implemented to separate systems and data based on sensitivity and criticality.
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols, and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management. Company uses firewalls and intrusion detection tools configured to prevent unauthorized access and detect external security threats.
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS/IPS deployed via Vercel. Firewalls and intrusion detection tools configured to prevent unauthorized access and detect external security threats.
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access via VPN/bastion host (referenced in network description). VPN access confirmed.
network_architecture.tls: Encryption technologies used to protect communication and transmission of data over public networks (TLS with certificates from third-party certificate authority)
network_architecture.content_filtering: Content filtering system routed through proxy server; endpoint protection includes email scanning
network_architecture.topology: Cluely Dashboard → Vercel (VPC, api platform) → Cloudflare (CDN/proxy) → Cloudflare Workers (queue 1, queue 2) → Neon (serverless Postgres database). Separate AWS Kubernetes cluster running: Deepgram Engine + Deepgram API + Deepgram License (3 instances). Cluely desktop app connects into the system. VPN/bastion host for remote admin access.

application_architecture.pattern: Multi-provider hybrid: Vercel (frontend/API hosting) + Cloudflare Workers (serverless edge queuing) + AWS Kubernetes (AI speech processing) + Neon (serverless database)
compute: Cloudflare Workers (edge serverless - queue 1 and queue 2)
AWS Kubernetes cluster (Deepgram Engine, Deepgram API, Deepgram License - 3 instances)
application_architecture.environments: At minimum: Production, Stage/Development (separate environments confirmed by CC8.1.4 - changes tested in separate development or test environment before implementation)

databases: Neon (serverless Postgres database - explicitly shown in network diagram as final data layer)
data_storage.backup_frequency: Not explicitly stated (Backup Policy exists with formal backup and recovery procedures; BCDR policy documented; no explicit daily backup frequency stated - SOC 2 Type 1 report has no CC7.4.2 backup frequency control)

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: Vercel (passwords controlled through 'Password policy of Vercel'); unique user IDs authenticated with associated passwords
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for all user accounts with administrative access to production platform (CC6.1.1); MFA required for source code tool access (CC8.1.3); 2FA confirmed for email access
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Vercel password policy enforced: periodic forced changes, password expiry and complexity requirements, account lockout after limited failed attempts

encryption.in_transit: TLS (version not specified; third-party CA certificates)
encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used to encrypt data at rest (CC6.1.5)
encryption.key_management: Cloud provider KMS (Vercel KMS); access to production environment access keys restricted to authorized individuals

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Git-based source code management tool (MFA required for access; changes logged, time-stamped, and attributed to author - CC8.1.3)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to require additional approval for every merge request moving change to production environment (CC8.1.2)
ci_cd_devops.iac: Configuration management tool used for baseline configurations, implementation, and rollback capability (tool not named - CC7.1.1)
ci_cd_devops.container_orchestration: Kubernetes (AWS cluster for Deepgram AI engines - visible in network diagram; not explicitly named as EKS or self-managed)
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management set up by CTO; monthly patch management reviews; workstations scanned for patch compliance daily; automatic OS updates configured (CC7.5.1)

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not named (monitoring tool generates alerts at predefined thresholds - CC5.2.1; IT team continuously monitors system capacity and performance)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled for admin activities, logon attempts, data deletions, function changes, security configurations, permissions, and roles at application and infrastructure level (CC7.2.1). Access to log configuration and modification is restricted.
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events; incident management process via ticketing application

third_party_services.purpose: Primary cloud infrastructure subservice organization - VPC, IDS/IPS, physical security of data center (carve-out); Vercel has own SOC 2 report
CDN/proxy layer and edge computing via Cloudflare Workers (queue processing) - shown in network diagram
Kubernetes cluster hosting Deepgram speech AI engines (Deepgram Engine, API, License) - shown in network diagram; not named as formal subservice organization
Serverless Postgres database - shown in network diagram as production data store
AI speech-to-text engine running within AWS Kubernetes cluster - 3 instances (Engine, API, License) visible in network diagram

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection software installed and configured on all production servers (continuous scanning, weekly AV definition updates for production servers) and Mac workstations/laptops (CC6.8.1)
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications; management reviews high/critical findings; remediation plan developed for critical and high vulnerabilities (CC7.1.2)
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party; high/critical findings remediated and tracked via incident management process (CC4.1.1)

excluded_criteria: Processing Integrity and Privacy trust services categories - Cluely has determined these are not relevant to the system
CC6.4 - Physical access controls: Vercel responsible for physical security of data center; excluded from scope
A1.2 - Environmental protections/backup/recovery: Cluely has no office or operates from working space managed by building management; all environmental security managed by building management/Vercel
notable_controls: Network diagram on page 18 explicitly shows four-provider architecture: Vercel (API platform) → Cloudflare (CDN/proxy + Workers with queue 1 and queue 2) → Neon (database); separate AWS Kubernetes cluster with Deepgram Engine/API/License
Deepgram as named AI speech engine - only company in cohort with explicit AI model vendor shown in network diagram
Neon as named serverless Postgres database provider - only company in cohort using Neon
Cloudflare Workers for edge queue processing - asynchronous processing architecture
Quarterly access reviews for all user accounts including privileged accounts (CC6.2.2)
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party (CC4.1.1)
BCDR tested annually with different loss scenarios
CISO role explicitly defined with responsibilities (CC7.4 / CC2.1.4)
Vendor management: annual SOC report reviews + quarterly risk assessments for critical vendors without SOC reports (CC9.2.1)

infrastructure.rationale: Most vendor-diverse architecture in the cohort: Vercel (hosting) + Cloudflare (CDN/edge) + AWS Kubernetes (AI processing) + Neon (database) all explicitly named in network diagram. Multi-AZ Vercel deployment confirmed. No multi-region failover. Four named providers is unique in this cohort. Vercel confirmed as having own SOC 2. The AWS and Cloudflare infrastructure is not acknowledged as formal subservice organizations despite being shown in the diagram.

application_architecture.rationale: Most technically detailed architecture diagram in the cohort for a SOC 2 Type 1 report: Vercel API platform + Cloudflare Workers (queue 1, queue 2) + AWS Kubernetes cluster + Neon database all explicitly shown. Kubernetes confirmed (AWS cluster). Separate environments confirmed. Branch protection and code review enforced. Cloudflare Workers for async queue processing is a sophisticated architectural pattern. No programming languages or frameworks named despite high infrastructure specificity.

data_layer.rationale: Neon (serverless Postgres) specifically named in network diagram - a meaningful positive signal and unique in the cohort. However, no explicit backup frequency stated (SOC 2 Type 1 report has no CC7.4.2 control). No caching layer named. No file storage named. No data warehouse. Single database technology. Multi-AZ confirmed via Vercel failover. Data classification and retention policies exist.

security_posture.rationale: Annual external pen test, monthly vulnerability scans, IDS/IPS via Vercel, firewalls, antivirus on all endpoints, MFA enforced across all access, KMS encryption at rest, quarterly access reviews. No WAF explicitly mentioned. No named SIEM. No cybersecurity insurance mentioned. Content filtering with email scanning is a slight positive differentiator. Solid fundamentals but missing key security visibility tools.

devops_maturity.rationale: Branch protection enforced, Kubernetes (AWS cluster) confirmed, separate environments, emergency change process, monthly patch reviews with automatic patching, BCDR tested annually. Cloudflare Workers suggest sophisticated async processing maturity. CI/CD tool not named, IaC tool not specifically named. Configuration management tool confirmed. The multi-provider architecture (4 vendors) requires higher DevOps maturity to operate.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented and reviewed annually. Annual testing performed with multiple loss scenarios. Multi-AZ failover by spinning up servers across all Vercel availability zones. No RTO/RPO disclosed. No explicit daily backup frequency stated (SOC 2 Type 1 limitation). No multi-region failover. Business impact analysis referenced.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Highest vendor diversity in the cohort: Vercel (hosting, carve-out with own SOC 2), Cloudflare (CDN/edge compute), AWS (Kubernetes/AI compute), Neon (database), and Deepgram (AI speech engine) all explicitly shown in the network diagram. Five named providers. Only Vercel formally named as subservice organization. The multi-vendor architecture reduces single-vendor dependency risk significantly compared to all other companies in this cohort.

overall.rationale: Cluely has the most vendor-diverse and architecturally explicit network diagram in this cohort: four named infrastructure providers (Vercel, Cloudflare, AWS, Neon) plus Deepgram as AI engine, all shown in the network diagram. This is a sophisticated multi-provider architecture appropriate for a real-time AI coaching application requiring low-latency speech processing. SOC 2 Type 1 (as of June 27, 2025) means only design suitability assessed - no operating effectiveness testing. No backup frequency disclosed, no RTO/RPO, no cybersecurity insurance mentioned. The multi-provider architecture is both a strength (diversity) and a risk (integration complexity and security surface area). Cloudflare Workers for queue processing and Deepgram for AI are uniquely specific disclosures not seen in other companies in the cohort.

red_flags: SOC 2 Type 1 only (not Type 2) - design suitability assessed but operating effectiveness not tested; no controls were actually tested over a period
No explicit backup frequency stated - Backup Policy exists but no daily backup frequency confirmed
No cybersecurity insurance mentioned (unlike other companies in the cohort)
AWS Kubernetes cluster and Cloudflare not listed as formal subservice organizations despite being shown in the network diagram and handling critical AI processing and data routing
yellow_flags: Only Vercel formally named as subservice organization - AWS, Cloudflare, Neon, and Deepgram handle critical functions but are not formally scoped as subservice organizations
No WAF mentioned despite operating a web-based SaaS platform (Vercel's built-in edge protection is not explicitly confirmed as WAF-equivalent)
Deepgram AI engines running on AWS represent an undisclosed AI model dependency - any Deepgram outage would likely break the core product
green_flags: Most technically specific network diagram in the cohort: five named services (Vercel, Cloudflare, Cloudflare Workers, Neon, AWS Kubernetes with Deepgram) explicitly shown
Neon (serverless Postgres) named as production database - unique in cohort
Deepgram named as AI speech engine - only company in cohort disclosing their AI model provider in the diagram
Cloudflare Workers for async queue processing - sophisticated edge computing architecture
Kubernetes (AWS cluster) for AI engine orchestration - container orchestration confirmed
Highest vendor diversity in the cohort - four infrastructure providers reduces single-vendor dependency
Cloudflare as CDN layer - the only company in the cohort with an explicit CDN shown in the network diagram
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party
Monthly automated vulnerability scanning with remediation plan for high/critical findings
MFA enforced across production platform, source code tools, and email
Quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts
Access revocation within one business day of termination
BCDR tested annually with multiple loss scenarios
Vercel confirmed to have its own SOC 2 report (subservice organization review)
CISO role explicitly defined with responsibilities for information security policy oversight
Vendor management: annual SOC reviews + quarterly risk assessments for critical vendors without SOC reports
Content filtering with email scanning implemented
Antivirus on all production servers (weekly definition updates) and Mac workstations
key_observations: Cluely's network diagram on page 18 is the most architecturally detailed in this cohort, explicitly showing five named providers: Vercel (API platform) → Cloudflare (CDN/proxy) → Cloudflare Workers (queue 1, queue 2) → Neon (serverless Postgres) as the primary data path, plus a separate AWS Kubernetes cluster running Deepgram Engine, Deepgram API, and Deepgram License (3 instances) for speech AI processing.

Cluely is the only company in this cohort with: (1) an explicit CDN in the network diagram (Cloudflare), (2) Cloudflare Workers for edge compute/queuing, (3) a named AI model vendor (Deepgram), and (4) a serverless Postgres database (Neon). This level of architectural specificity is unusual for a SOC 2 Type 1 report.

The async queue architecture (Cloudflare Workers queue 1 and queue 2) suggests speech processing is handled asynchronously - likely audio is captured, queued, then processed by Deepgram in the AWS cluster, with results returned to the user. This is appropriate for a real-time coaching use case where latency buffering matters.

Deepgram is not listed as a subservice organization despite running on AWS infrastructure and being the core AI capability. Any Deepgram outage would disable the product's primary function. This is a vendor risk that should be flagged in due diligence.

Neon is a serverless Postgres provider that runs on AWS. The combination of Neon + AWS Kubernetes + Cloudflare suggests the actual AWS footprint is larger than what Vercel covers as a subservice organization - the report likely underscopes AWS dependency.

The product targets sales professionals (video conferencing coaching) and integrates with all major enterprise video platforms. This B2B2C model means Cluely processes potentially sensitive business conversations - the privacy criteria exclusion ('not relevant to the system') is worth noting.

SOC 2 Type 1 as of June 27, 2025 is a point-in-time assessment. The company will need a Type 2 to demonstrate operating effectiveness. Given the complexity of a 4+ vendor architecture, the Type 2 will be a more significant undertaking.
Signed by Neel Shanmugam, COO (not CEO or CTO) - unusual signing authority relative to peers in this cohort who signed with CEO or CTO credentials.
